mappings.rationaleAI: Mexico is the direct subject of the entire A division, covering the Obregón-Calles era reforms, the Cárdenas Six-Year Plan, WWII cooperation with the US, and post-1945 political, economic, and social developments including PRI dominance, oil nationalisation, NAFTA, and the Fox election.

US relations with and interventions in Latin America run throughout section 974: US-Mexico relations and NAFTA (A.4.b), US military intervention in Nicaragua and support for Contras (B.1.d), US involvement in Panama (B.1.f), US occupation of Haiti (B.2.a), US trade embargo and Bay of Pigs invasion in Cuba (B.2.c.iv), and US efforts to subdue Colombian drug cartels (C.2.c).

Argentina is the direct subject of F.1, covering the conservative restoration (1930–43), the Perón era, post-Perón military dictatorships, the Falklands War, and return to civilian rule under Alfonsín and Menem.

Brazil is the direct subject of the entire G division, covering the Vargas dictatorship (1930–45), the constitutions of 1934 and 1937, post-war democratic and military periods through the Castelo Branco and successor presidencies, and the return to civilian rule under Sarney, Cardoso, and Lula.

The Cold War shaped Latin American politics via US-Soviet proxy conflicts: the Contra insurgency in Nicaragua sponsored by the US (B.1.d), Cuba's alignment with the Soviet bloc and Castro's support for leftist movements (B.2.c.iii), the Cuban missile crisis and Bay of Pigs (B.2.c.iv), and Venezuela's oil nationalization in the context of Cold War political economy (C.1.b).

The Great Depression is explicitly cited across multiple subsections as triggering economic crises and political instability: Bolivia's mining industry collapse and Chaco War context (D.3.a), Chile's economic crises of the 1930s (E.1.b), Uruguay's Depression-era dictatorship of Terra (F.2.a), Paraguay's Depression-era troubles (F.3.a), and the 1930 Brazilian revolution that brought Vargas to power (G.1).

Colombia is the direct subject of C.2, covering Liberal Party rule and López reforms (C.2.a), the Conservative restoration, La Violencia, and the National Front (C.2.b), and the era of drug trafficking and guerrilla violence under Betancur and successors (C.2.c).

Central America directly covers the subregion that is the subject of B.1, encompassing Guatemala, El Salvador, Nicaragua, Costa Rica, Panama, and Belize, including their colonial background (Spanish Captaincy-General of Guatemala) and 20th-century political trajectories.

WWII directly appears in A.3 (Mexico's wartime cooperation with the US and industrialisation), is referenced in F.1.a (Argentina's neutrality), F.2.a (Uruguay's wartime experience), F.3.a (Paraguay's Allied alignment), and G.1 (Brazil's Allied participation under Vargas).

Decolonisation is directly relevant to Belize's progress toward and achievement of independence from Britain in 1981 (B.1.g), the emergence of new independent Caribbean nations (B.2.d), and the status of remaining US and European territories in the Caribbean (B.2.e).
This article covers North American history including Mexico and Central America, directly relevant to section 974's A division (Mexico since 1920) and the Central American republics in B.1.

US imperialism and interventionism is a thread running through Nicaragua (B.1.d), Panama (B.1.f), Haiti (B.2.a), and Cuba (B.2.c.iv), all of which involve US military intervention or economic coercion — the core subject matter of imperialism theory.

The United Kingdom is directly relevant as the colonial power from which Belize gained independence (B.1.g), the metropolitan authority over British Caribbean possessions cited in B.2.e (Bermuda and other British insular possessions), and the context of Caribbean decolonisation in B.2.d.
The British Empire provides the colonial framework for Belize's independence process (B.1.g) and the British insular possessions listed in B.2.e (Bermuda and other British Caribbean territories).
